key_facts: On November 1, 2024, at approximately 00:54 AM, Plaintiff Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi was lawfully parked on a dirt shoulder off Interstate 580 East in Livermore, California, resting inside a rental Tesla Model Y.

Plaintiff had pulled over out of an abundance of caution after feeling fatigued following a long night of social activity, consistent with his professional training as a commercial truck driver where safety and rest are paramount.
Plaintiff was not engaged in any unlawful conduct and posed no threat to public safety at the time of the encounter.
CHP Officers Santiago Meza-Gonzalez (#023295) and Officer Melano (#023054) approached Plaintiff's vehicle under the pretext of a welfare check.

Upon approaching the vehicle, one officer remarked, 'It's a rental,' and upon observing a cup in the center console, stated, 'I think it's booze,' to which the other officer replied, 'Yeah?' — marking a shift from welfare concern to investigative suspicion of DUI.
Officers turned their flashlights into the windows and began peering into the vehicle in an investigatory manner without Plaintiff's consent or a warrant.
Officers speculated that a cup inside the vehicle might contain 'booze' without any specific or articulable facts.
Officers proceeded to open Plaintiff's passenger door without consent, probable cause, or exigent circumstances.

Plaintiff was ordered out of his vehicle despite having remained calm, compliant, and cooperative throughout the encounter, making no threats, no sudden movements, and posing no apparent risk to officer safety.
Plaintiff was questioned extensively and subjected to field sobriety tests, despite exhibiting no signs of criminal activity or intoxication.

Officer Melano conducted an intrusive search of Plaintiff's vehicle without consent or probable cause, including reaching into the cupholders, examining the center console area, and scanning around the footwells and floorboards of the vehicle.
At no point did Officer Melano have any specific or articulable facts justifying the belief that Plaintiff's vehicle contained contraband or evidence of a crime.
No evidence of intoxication or illegal activity was found.
Plaintiff was detained, placed in handcuffs, and transported for chemical testing under suspicion of DUI, without sufficient probable cause.

Despite the arrest report reflecting that charges were sought, the California Highway Patrol later issued correspondence characterizing the encounter as a mere 'detention' because no charges were filed by the District Attorney.

Plaintiff was subjected to handcuffing, placed in the back of a patrol vehicle, transported under guard, and read chemical test advisements — all indicia of a full custodial arrest within the meaning of Dunaway v. New York, 442 U.S. 200 (1979).
The administrative chemical refusal designation triggered an extended suspension of Plaintiff's Class A commercial driver's license until February 2026.

As a result of the CDL suspension, Plaintiff lost access to steady employment in the trucking industry, leading to more than a year of lost wages, reduced earning capacity, and a downward shift in financial stability.

Plaintiff experienced sustained anxiety, frustration, and feelings of helplessness and isolation, compounded by the stigma of a DUI-related designation that continues to impact his reputation and employment prospects.
The arrest triggered a parole violation hold, and Plaintiff was incarcerated from the Friday of the incident until the following Wednesday.
During incarceration, Plaintiff was not brought to court for arraignment and was forced to share a confined space with an individual facing homicide charges.

Plaintiff was subjected to verbal hostility and intimidation, including threats and visible agitation from the other detainee, who became increasingly angry as Plaintiff questioned the reasons for his continued detention.
Plaintiff feared for his safety and well-being, suffering unnecessary trauma and exposure to volatile and dangerous conditions as a result of Defendants' actions.

The constitutional violations were alleged to have been caused by customs, policies, or practices of the California Highway Patrol and/or its deliberate indifference to citizens' constitutional rights.

dates.event: Incident — Plaintiff parked on dirt shoulder off I-580 East in Livermore, CA; officers approach, conduct warrantless search, detain and arrest Plaintiff
Plaintiff incarcerated following parole violation hold triggered by arrest (released following Wednesday)
dates.date: Friday, November 1, 2024 through approximately Wednesday, November 5 or 6, 2024

dates.event: Plaintiff's Class A commercial driver's license suspension set to expire
Complaint signed/dated by Plaintiff (In Pro Per)
Complaint filed with the U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California

parties: Plaintiff: Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi, resident of California, 1455 Cat Tail Drive, Stockton, CA 95204; commercial truck driver; proceeding In Pro Per
Defendant: State of California — government entity employing CHP officers
Defendant: California Highway Patrol — division of the State of California responsible for training, oversight, and conduct of its officers
Defendant: Officer Santiago Meza-Gonzalez, Badge #023295 — California Highway Patrol Officer, sued in both individual and official capacities
Defendant: Officer Melano, Badge #023054 — California Highway Patrol Officer, sued in both individual and official capacities
Defendants: DOES 1 through 10 — presently unknown persons who participated in the violations described
causes_of_action: Count I: Violation of the Fourth Amendment (U.S. Constitution) — Unlawful Search and Seizure under 42 U.S.C. § 1983; Defendants conducted an unlawful search and seizure without probable cause, consent, exigent circumstances, or a valid warrant

Count II: Violation of the Fourteenth Amendment (U.S. Constitution) — Due Process under 42 U.S.C. § 1983; Defendants violated Plaintiff's due process rights by arbitrarily and unlawfully detaining him and escalating a welfare check into an unwarranted criminal investigation

Count III: Municipal Liability — Monell Claim Against State of California and California Highway Patrol under 42 U.S.C. § 1983; constitutional violations caused by customs, policies, or practices of the CHP and/or its deliberate indifference to citizens' constitutional rights
evidence_cited: Rental Tesla Model Y — vehicle Plaintiff was resting in at time of the stop
Cup in the center console of the vehicle — speculated by officers to contain 'booze,' forming the alleged pretext for the DUI investigation
CHP arrest report — reflected that charges were sought against Plaintiff
CHP correspondence characterizing the encounter as a 'detention' (issued after no charges were filed by the District Attorney)
Chemical test advisements — read to Plaintiff during custodial transport, constituting indicia of a full custodial arrest
Administrative chemical refusal designation — triggered CDL suspension until February 2026
Class A commercial driver's license suspension record — documentary evidence of economic harm
Field sobriety test results — no signs of intoxication or criminal activity found
Chemical test results — no evidence of intoxication or illegal activity found
relief_sought: Plaintiff requests: (1) compensatory damages in an amount to be proven at trial; (2) punitive damages against individual Defendants; (3) reasonable attorneys' fees and costs pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1988; (4) any such further relief as the Court deems just and proper. Plaintiff also demands a jury trial on all issues so triable.
notable_quotes: 'It's a rental.' — officer's remark upon approaching Plaintiff's vehicle
'I think it's booze.' — officer's statement upon observing a cup in the center console
'Yeah?' — second officer's reply, marking the shift from welfare check to DUI investigation
'detention' — characterization used by CHP in post-incident correspondence to describe the encounter, disputed by Plaintiff as legally and factually incorrect
legal_standards_cited: 42 U.S.C. § 1983 — basis for federal civil rights claims
U.S. Constitution, Fourth Amendment — protection against unreasonable searches and seizures
U.S. Constitution, Fourteenth Amendment — due process

Dunaway v. New York, 442 U.S. 200 (1979) — standard for what constitutes a full custodial arrest (handcuffing, transport under guard, reading of chemical test advisements all constitute indicia of arrest)
Monell liability standard — municipal/entity liability for constitutional violations caused by official customs, policies, or practices, or deliberate indifference
42 U.S.C. § 1988 — attorneys' fees in civil rights actions
procedural_significance: This is the initiating pleading in the action, filed pro se on May 27, 2025, which frames all claims, establishes the factual record, and defines the scope of constitutional violations alleged against both individual officers and the CHP as an entity; it is the foundational document against which all subsequent motions, answers, and discovery will be measured.
